coredata.dc:title: In situ growth of 2D/3D Bi2MoO6/CeO2 heterostructures toward enhanced photodegradation and Cr(VI) reduction
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Qiang
Sun, Kun
Cui, Yuchen
Wang, Shuaijun
Liu, Chenguang
Liu, Bin
coredata.dc:description: The construction of semiconductor-based photocatalysts has attracted more and more attention due to their great potential for the utilization of solar light. In our work, a heterostructure by in situ integration of bismuth molybdate nanosheets and ceria microspheres (Bi2MoO6/CeO2) was successfully fabricated and used for effective photocatalytic degradation of pollutants and Cr(VI) reduction. The chemical compositions, microstructures, and optical properties of obtained nanocomposites were investigated by various characterization. The optimized Bi2MoO6/CeO2 showed more superior degradation rate for RhB and tetracycline than the most Bi2MoO6-based photocatalysts previously reported, and exhibited superb removal rates of 98.4% for Cr(VI) reduction within 90 min, which was much higher than those of pristine Bi2MoO6 and CeO2. A typical type–II photocatalytic mechanism was put forward by considering active species capture experiments, EPR, and XPS analysis, which was beneficial to adjust the bandgap structure and accelerate charge separation. This present work manifests that Bi2MoO6/CeO2 nanocomposites with excellent photocatalytic properties have a vast application prospect in wastewater remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi2MoO6/CeO2
Nanocomposite
Photocatalysts
Degradation
Mechanism

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic explanation for formation process of CeO2 microsphere and Bi2MoO6/CeO2 composite.
XRD patterns (a), FT–IR spectra (b), nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm curves (c), and pore size distributions (d) of the synthesized catalysts.
SEM images of CeO2 (a), Bi2MoO6 (b), BC–1 (c–d), TEM images (e–g) and EDX elemental mapping images (h) of BC–1 composite.
(a) XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) Ce 3d, (c) Bi 4f and (d) Mo 3d from CeO2, Bi2MoO6, and BC-1 composite.
UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra (a), evaluation of band–gap energy (b–c), Mott-Schottky curve (d–e), and band gap structure (f) of the synthesized samples.
Photocatalytic removal efficiency for RhB degradation (a) and kinetics curves (b), photocatalytic removal efficiency for Cr (VI) reduction (c), and UV–vis absorption spectra of aqueous Cr(VI) (d).
Reusability performance of BC–1 composite for RhB degradation (a) and Cr(VI) reduction (b), XPS spectra (c, d) of BC–1 composite before and after photocatalytic reaction.
(a) Photocurrent, (b) EIS, (c) photoluminescence spectra, (d) degradation efficiency for RhB under different scavengers, and (e–f) EPR spectra of the synthesized catalysts.
Proposed photocatalytic mechanism for BC–1 composite under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: In situ growth of 2D/3D Bi2MoO6/CeO2 heterostructures toward enhanced photodegradation and Cr(VI) reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qinghai Normal University, Xining 810008, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qinghai Normal University, Xining 810008, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Power Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Energy and Power Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The construction of semiconductor-based photocatalysts has attracted more and more attention due to their great potential for the utilization of solar light. In our work, a heterostructure by in situ integration of bismuth molybdate nanosheets and ceria microspheres (Bi2MoO6/CeO2) was successfully fabricated and used for effective photocatalytic degradation of pollutants and Cr(VI) reduction. The chemical compositions, microstructures, and optical properties of obtained nanocomposites were investigated by various characterization. The optimized Bi2MoO6/CeO2 showed more superior degradation rate for RhB and tetracycline than the most Bi2MoO6-based photocatalysts previously reported, and exhibited superb removal rates of 98.4% for Cr(VI) reduction within 90 min, which was much higher than those of pristine Bi2MoO6 and CeO2. A typical type–II photocatalytic mechanism was put forward by considering active species capture experiments, EPR, and XPS analysis, which was beneficial to adjust the bandgap structure and accelerate charge separation. This present work manifests that Bi2MoO6/CeO2 nanocomposites with excellent photocatalytic properties have a vast application prospect in wastewater remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decade, the threats of water pollution to global environment has become more and more serious with the rapid development of industrialization [1–3]. There are many factors causing water pollution, mainly involving dye wastewater, antibiotics, phenolic compounds, and heavy metal ions. It is undoubtedly a great challenge to develop new and effective approaches for the remediation of contaminants from wastewater. Solar–driven photocatalysis based on semiconductor materials has been regarded as a new approach with enormous prospects to deal with environmental pollution and energy shortage in recent years [4,5]. However, a large number of studies have proved that the decomposition efficiency of pollutants during photocatalytic degradation process was unsatisfactory due to the poor light absorption efficiency and high electron–hole recombination rate when only single–component semiconductor materials existed. Meanwhile, the researchers have carried out extensive research on the design of heterojunction photocatalysts between different materials in the past few years [6–8]. Theoretically, suited band structures and close interfacial contact are the primary factors to ensure the rapid transfer of photogenerated carriers, and thus enhancing the photocatalytic capacity [9]. Therefore, it is necessary to construct heterojunction photocatalysts with matched nanostructures and interfaces to remove various pollutants under illumination.

Bismuth oxide–type photocatalysts, as a kind of widely used semiconductor materials, have received enormous attention due to unique bandgap structure and remarkable photoelectrical properties [10–12]. Particularly, bismuth molybdate (Bi2MoO6) with a suitable band gap energy of approximately 2.7 eV has gained tremendous research interest due to the strong visible light response and unique nanostructures [13]. Especially, two–dimensional (2D) Bi2MoO6 nanosheets have larger specific surface area, more active sites and shorter charge diffusion pathway, which could promote the transfer and separation of photogenerated e-–h+ pairs and contribute to photocatalytic activity [14]. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic capability of mono–component Bi2MoO6 was seriously limited by the poor quantum yield due to the rapid recombination of photoinduced charge carriers, which also restrained the application in practical wastewater treatment. Up to now, many studies have proved that the redox ability of the photocatalysts could be promoted by integrating 2D Bi2MoO6 and other photocatalytic materials to establish heterojunction structure. For instance, Guo et al. [15] fabricated a CuInS2/Bi2MoO6 heterostructure for photodegradation by in–situ hydrothermal reactions, and 84.7% of tetracycline was removed within 120 min under illumination. The enhanced separation rate of photogenerated e-–h+ was revealed by the formation of direct Z–scheme heterostructure with suitable band structure. Zhang et al. [16] also synthesized hierarchical Bi2MoO6/ZnO nanocomposite on the basis of charge transfer theory, and shown significant potential of photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI). Therefore, it is conceivable that the design of Bi2MoO6-based heterojunctions demonstrates a promising prospect for developing a novel photocatalyst.

Ceria (CeO2) as a class of rare earth oxides was regarded as a typical photoactive component based on its excellent properties, including wide bandgap structure (about 2.8 ∼ 3.1 eV), high oxygen storage capacity, remarkable catalytic activity, and low cost. It was generally accepted that the energy level structure and charge transport pathway of semiconductors were significant affected by the controlled morphology [17]. So far, CeO2 materials with different nanostructures have been successfully synthesized, including nanorods [18], nanowires [19], nanocubes [20], nanosheets [21,22], and nanospheres [23,24]. Especially, CeO2 with 3D nanosphere structure has excellent photocatalytic performance by integrating with appropriate cocatalysts due to the dual roles of catalyst and carrier. For example, Liu et al. [25] fabricated g–C3N4@CeO2 core–shell nanocomposite by hydrothermal method for the degradation of antibiotic doxycycline hydrochloride in combination with H2O2 under illumination, and the significant photocatalytic degradation efficiency was mainly attributed to the strong interactions and effective charge separation between CeO2 nanosphere and g-C3N4 thin layer. Besides, although some previous studies on the integration of Bi2MoO6 and CeO2 have exhibited satisfactory photocatalytic capacity [26,27], the synthesis approach of composite materials still suffered from complex process and long time. Hence, the skillful combination of 2D Bi2MoO6 nanosheets and 3D CeO2 nanospheres into composites by a simple strategy could effectively improve the band structure and promote the separation of photogenerated charge, thus further enhancing the photocatalytic performance.

Herein, Bi2MoO6/CeO2 nanomaterials were successfully prepared by in situ growth of Bi2MoO6 nanosheets on CeO2 microspheres surface. The proposed heterojunction exhibited excellent photocatalytic capacity for the contaminants degradation and Cr(VI) reduction under light irradiation, which might be due to the formation of heterojunction by the close contact between Bi2MoO6 and CeO2, effectively suppressing the recombination of photogenerated e-–h+ pairs. The chemical compositions, microstructures, optical properties, photocatalytic performance, and stability of as–synthesized composites were thoroughly characterized and discussed. The reactive species generated by binary Bi2MoO6/CeO2 nanocomposites during photocatalytic process were detected and the dominant role was determined by quenching experiments and EPR technique. Also, a possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism was proposed.

For the preparation of CeO2, 1.3 g Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and 3.6 g CO(NH2)2 were dissolved in 80 mL deionized water and stirred constantly for 30 min, then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon–lined autoclaves and sealed at 120 °C for 3 h. After cooling down to room temperature, white precipitate was separated and washed with deionized water and ethanol three times and then dried at 60 °C for 5 h. Finally, the CeO2 powder was obtained by directly calcination at 400 °C for 2 h.

Bi2MoO6/CeO2 (BC) was synthesized by solvothermal procedure as schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 . Typically, 1 mmol CeO2 microsphere was dispersed in ethanol (30 mL) and sonicated for 20 min to obtain solution A. Meanwhile, 2 mmol Bi(NO3)3··5H2O and 1 mmol Na2MoO4··2H2O were completely dissolved in ethylene glycol (30 mL) to obtain solution B. Then the solution A was mixed into solution B rapidly and stirred continuously for 1 h. Subsequently, the suspended solution was transferred to Teflon–lined stainless steel autoclave and maintained at 160 °C for 12 h. After the hydrothermal treatment, the product denoted as BC-1 was collected by centrifugation and washed three times with distilled water, finally dried at 80 °C for 12 h. For clarity purpose, BC-0.5, BC-2, and BC-3 were prepared by using different amount of CeO2, where the number was the molar ratio of Bi2MoO6/CeO2. For comparison, pristine Bi2MoO6 was synthesized by the same conditions except adding CeO2. The details for nanocomposites characterization, performance test and analytical methods were in Supporting Information.

The compositions and phase structures of Bi2MoO6, CeO2, and Bi2MoO6/CeO2 samples with diverse amount of CeO2 were determined by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the diffraction peaks of CeO2 and Bi2MoO6 were related to fluorite CeO2 (JCPDF: 43-1002) [28] and orthorhombic Bi2MoO6 (JCPDS: 21-0102) [15]. In particular, the diffraction peaks of 28.6°, 33.1°, 47.6°, and 56.4° were indexed to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of CeO2, while the characteristic peaks at 28.3°, 32.5°, 46.7°, 55.4°, and 58.5°were corresponded to the (131), (200), (202), (331), and (262) crystal planes of Bi2MoO6. Meanwhile, all characteristic diffraction peaks of CeO2 and Bi2MoO6 were observed in the resultant Bi2MoO6/CeO2 composites and no additional impurity were found, suggesting the successful fabrication of Bi2MoO6/CeO2 composites by one-step solvothermal process. Although the diffraction peaks of CeO2 and Bi2MoO6 were too close to identify, the peak intensity of Bi2MoO6/CeO2 composites gradually enhanced with the increasing amounts of Bi2MoO6.

The FT–IR spectra of CeO2, Bi2MoO6, and BC–1 composite over the wavenumber range from 4000 to 400 cm−1 were presented in Fig. 2b. All samples showed broad peaks at around 3430 cm−1 and 1310 cm−1, which were due to the stretching and deformation vibrations of O–H groups in the adsorbed H2O molecules [29]. The characteristic peak at 1623 cm−1 was assigned to CO skeletal vibrations of carboxyl group [30,31]. The typical vibration for pure CeO2 observed at 1538 cm−1, 1062 cm−1, and below 1000 cm−1 with all absorption peaks appeared in proper position [32,33]. In the spectrum of pristine Bi2MoO6, the absorption peaks at 950–700 cm−1 (such as 840 and 735 cm−1) were related with Mo–O vibration, while the bands between 600 and 400 cm−1 could be coincident with the stretching vibration of Bi–O bond [34,35]. In addition, the synthesized BC–1 composite shown consistent characteristic peaks corresponding to CeO2 and Bi2MoO6, suggesting the successful integration of Bi2MoO6 nanosheets onto CeO2 microspheres.

Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms of CeO2, Bi2MoO6, and BC-1 composite were exhibited in Fig. 2c. All the as-synthesized catalysts appeared the property of mesoporous with typical type-IV isotherms in the relative pressure range of 0.8–1.0, which verified the existence of mesopores in the synthesized samples [36]. The curves of pore size distributions (Fig. 2d) reflected that all the samples have a characteristic of unimodal distribution, and the corresponding average pore sizes were presented in Tab. S1. Besides, the BET surface area of BC-1 composite was obviously increased compared with the pristine Bi2MoO6, implying that Bi2MoO6 nanosheets were well exposed to the surface of CeO2 microspheres after integration. It has been generally accepted that large specific surface area was beneficial to the increase of active sites and the decrease of mass transfer resistance for pollutant adsorption, which contributed to excellent catalytic performance.

The microstructures of prepared samples with different magnification were further investigated by SEM, TEM, and HRTEM. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, pure CeO2 shown a regular globular structure with a diameter of about 500 nm. The structure of pure Bi2MoO6 was flower–shaped microsphere with the diameter of about 1 ∼ 2 μm, which was formed by many nanosheets (Fig. 3b and Fig. S1). Notably, a 2D/3D heterostructure of BC–1 composite as shown in Fig. 3c and 3d was constructed by the integration that Bi2MoO6 nanosheets were uniformly coated on surface of CeO2 microspheres, while was consistent with the results of BET surface area. Meanwhile, the TEM and HRTEM images (Fig. 3e ∼ g) of BC–1 photocatalyst could also reflect the close interface contact between Bi2MoO6 and CeO2, which greatly promoted the charge transfer efficiency [37]. In addition, it could be clearly seen from Fig. 3f that the lattice spacings of Bi2MoO6 and CeO2 over BC–1 composite were 0.32 and 0.31 nm, respectively [38,39]. The corresponding EDX elemental mapping analysis was obtained and the results were presented in Fig. 3h. The uniform elemental distribution of Ce, O, Bi, and Mo in BC–1 composite further confirmed the successful construction of Bi2MoO6/CeO2 heterojunction.

The elemental compositions and chemical states of CeO2, Bi2MoO6, and BC–1 nanohybrid were further characterized by XPS. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, Ce, Bi, Mo, and O were contained in survey spectrum, indicating the coexistence of Bi2MoO6 and CeO2 in BC–1 composite. Fig. 4b-d shown the high resolution XPS spectrum of Ce 3d, Bi 4f, and Mo 3d in the CeO2, Bi2MoO6, and BC–1 composite. It could be seen from Fig. 4b that the XPS signal intensity of Ce in BC–1 was much weaker than that of pristine CeO2, which was not difficult to understand since most of CeO2 microspheres were wrapped by Bi2MoO6 nanosheets as described in the SEM images. Meanwhile, eight signal peaks were detected from the high resolution XPS spectrum of Ce 3d in BC–1 composite (Fig. S2a). The peaks at 916.7, 907.6, 903.5, and 900.8 eV were ascribed to Ce 3d3/2 and Ce 3d5/2 centered at 898.1, 888.7, 884.7, and 882.1 eV. Besides, the peaks of binding energy at 884.7 eV and 903.5 eV were corresponded to Ce3+ species, while others were attributed to the Ce4+ [40]. This result suggested that both Ce3+ and Ce4+ appeared in BC–1 composite. In the Bi 4f spectrum (Fig. 4c), the characteristic peaks of Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of Bi3+ species were observed at 159.0 and 164.3 eV, respectively [41]. For Mo element, the spectrum in Fig. 4d consisted of two individual peaks at 235.4 (Mo 3d3/2) and 232.2 (Mo 3d5/2) correspond to the oxidation state of molybdenum [42]. The O 1 s spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks at 529.9, 531.5, and 533.3 eV (Fig. S2b), which could be indexed to the orbital splits of Ce–O, Bi–O, and surface adsorbed oxygen of photocatalysts [43]. Moreover, it was noteworthy that the binding energies of Ce 3d for BC–1 composite were higher than those of pure CeO2, and the peaks of Bi 4f and Mo 3d in the BC–1 composite also shifted towards the lower binding energies in comparison with bare Bi2MoO6. These results indicated that photogenerated electrons were transferred from the surface of CeO2 to that of Bi2MoO6 in the BC–1 composite, which could significantly promote the separation of photogenerated e-−h+ and further improve the photocatalytic performance [16,44].

The optical absorption capacity of as-synthesized samples was investigated by UV–vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy analysis. As depicted in Fig. 5 a, the pristine Bi2MoO6 with an absorption band edge near 460 nm shown a stronger absorption than CeO2, and all the as–synthesized BC samples with a similar absorption band edge to Bi2MoO6 exhibited a stronger absorption capability to visible light after the growth of Bi2MoO6 nanosheets on the CeO2 surface, indicating the formation of heterojunction by the close interaction between CeO2 and Bi2MoO6. Additionally, according to the Kubelka-Munk equation, the bandgap energies were calculated to be about 2.75 and 2.69 eV for CeO2 and Bi2MoO6 (Fig. 5b and 5c).

Mott–Schottky (M−S) curves were further analyzed to determine the electron density and conductive band potential of semiconductors and the results were shown in Fig. 5d and 5e. Positive slopes in the M−S curves indicated that both CeO2 and Bi2MoO6 were typical n–type semiconductors [45]. The obtained flat band positions for CeO2 and Bi2MoO6 were at −0.86 and −0.39 eV vs SCE, corresponding to −0.62 and −0.15 eV vs NHE, respectively. In general, the conduction band (CB) potential of n-type semiconductors is negative 0.2 eV compared to flat band position [46]. Hence, the CB potential positions of CeO2 and Bi2MoO6 were at −0.82 and −0.35 eV, respectively. Meanwhile, the corresponding valence band potential (VB) of CeO2 and Bi2MoO6 were estimated to be 1.93 and 2.34 eV [47]. The energy band structure obtained was shown in Fig. 5f, and there was a well-matched band structure between CeO2 and Bi2MoO6, which could be determined from the fact that CeO2 has more negative potential position of CB and VB than Bi2MoO6. More importantly, this band structure could effectively promote the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers and lead to excellent photocatalytic capacity.

The photodegradation performance of as–synthesized samples under visible light illumination were studied by the elimination of RhB as contaminant. It can be seen from Fig. 6 a that the photocatalytic degradation capacity of pristine CeO2 and Bi2MoO6 were insufficient, and the removal efficiency for RhB within 90 min were only 8.8% and 34.1%, respectively. The main reasons for this phenomenon were attributed to the fast recombination of photogenerated charges. Surprisingly, the removal efficiency for RhB was significantly improved after the combination of CeO2 and Bi2MoO6, and have the order of BC-1＞BC-2＞BC-3＞BC-0.5. A possible reason for excellent performance was the formation of abundant heterojunctions between CeO2 and Bi2MoO6 [48]. In addition, the presence of intimate interfacial contact between different nanostructures also contributed to efficient charge transfer across the surfaces, which was beneficial to prevent the rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers. Moreover, BC–1 composite exhibited the highest apparent rate constant for RhB removal, about 7.2 and 32.0 times than those of Bi2MoO6 and CeO2, respectively (Fig. 6b). Besides, TC as a kind of widely used antibiotics was also used as a target pollutant to further investigate the photocatalytic performance of BC–1 composite, and a similar result was shown in Fig. S3a. The TC removal efficiency over BC–1 was up to 94.1% during 90 min reaction, which was much higher than those of CeO2 and Bi2MoO6. The corresponding apparent rate constant of BC–1 composite was also significantly higher than that of two individual components (Fig. S3b). To our knowledge, the remarkable degradation capacity was superior to the previously reported Bi2MoO6–based materials in the treatment of most refractory organic contaminants (Tab. S2).

The photocatalytic Cr (VI) reduction tests were carried out to further evaluate the application potential of Bi2MoO6/CeO2 composites. As shown in Fig. 6c, the Cr(VI) reduction efficiency of pure Bi2MoO6 was compared and only 45.6% Cr(VI) was reduced during 90 min irradiation. Obviously, about 98.4% reduction efficiency of Cr(VI) was achieved by BC-1 composite, which was much better than that of single component. A possible explanation behind this, the formation of heterojunction between Bi2MoO6 and CeO2 greatly promoted the separation of photogenerated e-−h+, which was conducive to the superior reduction ability of electrons located in the conduction band. In addition, Fig. 6d shown the UV–vis absorbance curve of aqueous Cr(VI) at different reaction time points. The absorption peak of Cr(VI) gradually disappeared with increasing illumination time, suggesting that Cr(VI) was continuously reduced during the photocatalytic process. In general, the efficient construction of heterojunction between Bi2MoO6 and CeO2 contributed significantly to the enhancement of redox efficiency.

The cyclic experiments were conducted to further investigate the stability of BC–1 composite, and the results were shown in Fig. 7 a and b. It could be clearly seen that the photocatalytic efficiency for RhB degradation and Cr(VI) reduction hardly decreased after six consecutive cycles, and the recycled BC–1 composite was further characterized by SEM and XRD. It could be seen that the 2D/3D structure of as–synthesized photocatalyst was still intact (Fig. S4a and S4b), and the XRD patterns of BC–1 sample did not obviously change except peak intensity before and after the cyclic tests (Fig. S4c). Meanwhile, the low concentration of leaching ions (Bi3+ and Mo6+) in solution after photocatalytic reaction was also monitored by AAS technique (Tab. S3). All these results indicated the excellent stability of BC–1 composite. In addition, the mineralization degree of RhB by BC–1 composite in each cycle experiment was also analyzed. It was observed from Fig. 7a that the TOC removal efficiency of RhB was still reached about 73.8% after six runs, suggesting that the contaminants could be effectively mineralized. The elemental composition and valence state of BC–1 composite before and after photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) were characterized by XPS. As shown in Fig. 7c and d, Cr element was found in the XPS spectra of recycled sample and two strong peaks with the binding energy located at 586.6 eV and 576.9 eV could be observed, which attributed to Cr 2p1/2 and Cr 2p3/2 of Cr(III) [49,50]. It could be inferred that the absence of Cr(VI) species confirmed the complete conversion of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) by photocatalytic reaction.

Besides, the photocatalytic RhB degradation and Cr(VI) reduction in different water matrices were also carried out to further evaluate the adaptability to actual water matrix. As displayed in Fig. S5a, the removal efficiency of RhB shown a decreasing trend and the kinetic rate constants in deionized water and tap water were 0.042 and 0.023 min−1, respectively. The suppression effect of RhB degradation in tap water mainly attributed to the competition of organic matters for radicals and the adsorption on the surface of photocatalyst [51]. In contrast, the Cr(VI) reduction rate in tap water was slightly higher than that in deionized water (Fig. S5b), which might be due to the depletion of photogenerated holes by organic molecules and more photogenerated electrons participated in the Cr(VI) reduction process [52].

It was generally accepted that effective suppression for the rapid recombination of photoexcited e-–h+ pairs was a key point to improve photocatalytic performance. Therefore, transient photocurrent response, EIS, and PL were further measured to evaluate the charge separation efficiency of CeO2, Bi2MoO6, and BC–1. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the photocurrent response intensity of BC–1 composite was obviously higher than that of two individual units, which indicated that BC–1 composite has more efficient charge separation and transfer [53]. Similarly, the BC–1 composite exhibited more smaller radius of the arc as compared with CeO2 and Bi2MoO6 in EIS curves (Fig. 8b), indicating its superior charge transfer efficiency [54]. Additionally, the high peak intensity of photoluminescence curves for pure CeO2 and Bi2MoO6 confirmed the larger recombination probability of photogenerated charges (Fig. 8c). These results implied that the formation of heterostructure in BC–1 composite successfully suppressed the recombination of photogenerated e-–h+ pairs, which contributed to the improvement of its photocatalytic performance.

To identify the main active radicals during the photocatalytic degradation process for RhB, quenching experiments were carried out. As shown in Fig. 8d, the degradation efficiency of RhB decreased significantly in the presence of BQ, IPA, and AO as scavengers, indicating the generation of •O2 −, •OH, and h+ in the photocatalytic system [55]. Especially, the strongest suppression effect on degradation efficiency (from 98.1% to 53.4%) was found when adding AO. These results suggested that h+ played a key role in the degradation process.

To further elucidate the generation and evolution of radical species by BC–1 composite under irradiation, signals of DMPO−•OH and DMPO−•O2 − were collected by EPR measurement. As described in Fig. 8e and 8f, there were no obvious signal peaks in the absence of visible light, indicating that radicals were only generated under irradiation. Specifically, the signal of DMPO−•OH adduct with a classical four–line spectrum in Fig. 8e was detected for BC–1 composite after 10 min illumination, which indicated the existence of •OH in the photocatalytic process [56]. Meanwhile, the signal peak of DMPO−•O2 − confirmed reflected the formation of •O2 − in the BC–1 photocatalytic system (Fig. 8f) [57]. In general, the construction of heterojunction by the close contact between Bi2MoO6 and CeO2 effectively suppressed the recombination of photogenerated e-–h+ pairs and generated oxidizing species under visible light irradiation, which significantly enhanced the photocatalytic activity.

Ultimately, a plausible photocatalytic mechanism of type–II heterojunction was proposed based on the results above, as shown in Fig. 9 . The photoexcited electrons (e-) of both Bi2MoO6 and CeO2 were generated and then immediately transmitted from the CB of CeO2 to the CB of Bi2MoO6 because of the more negative CB edge potential of CeO2. Furthermore, the photo–induced holes (h+) remaining at VB of Bi2MoO6 also migrated rapidly to VB of CeO2, which could exert strong oxidation and directly degrade the organic pollutants. Nevertheless, it was weak for the reduction of surface adsorbed oxygen to •O2 − by the electrons accumulated on CB of Bi2MoO6 due to the similar potential values between Bi2MoO6 (−0.35 eV) and O2/•O2 − (−0.33 eV). The •OH radicals produced by the conversion of •O2 − was also insignificant. These inferences were in agreement with the results of trapping experiment. Meanwhile, Cr(VI) was successfully reduced to Cr(III) by the accumulated electrons on the Bi2MoO6 surface. As a result, the photogenerated electrons and holes in BC–1 composite were effectively separated and the photocatalytic capacity was effectively improved.

In this study, Bi2MoO6/CeO2 composite was successfully constructed by one-step solvothermal synthesis and applied for photodegradation and Cr(VI) reduction. The optimal BC–1 composite shown superior photocatalytic removal efficiency and remarkable reusability for RhB degradation and Cr(VI) reduction. A type–II heterojunction with close interfacial contact was designed by in situ growth of Bi2MoO6 nanosheets on CeO2 microspheres, which was responsible for adjusting the visible light response and accelerating charge separation, thus enhancing the photocatalytic performance. This present study provides a promising strategy for the rational design and environmentally friendly application of photocatalytic materials with multidimensional nanostructures.

Qiang Gao: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Kun Sun: Data curation, Visualization, Formal analysis. Yuchen Cui: Visualization, Formal analysis. Shuaijun Wang: Investigation, Visualization. Chenguang Liu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Bin Liu: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21808243 and 22078362).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2021.120312.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
